draft.introduction: Book VIII of Augustine’s De Civitate Dei (c. 413–426 CE) engages the Platonists as the most formidable pagan philosophers. Augustine grants that Plato came closest to Christian truth but argues that even the best pagan philosophy fails without the Incarnation.

translation.text: If Plato said that the wise man is he who imitates, knows, and loves this God, and that participation in this God makes one blessed, what need is there to examine the other philosophers? None have come nearer to us than the Platonists.
translation.original_text: Si ergo Plato Dei huius imitatorem cognitorem amatorem dixit esse sapientem, cuius participatione sit beatus, quid opus est excutere ceteros? Nulli nobis quam isti propius accesserunt.

translator_notes.note: Augustine’s praise of the Platonists—“none have come nearer to us” (nulli nobis quam isti propius accesserunt)—is both an embrace and a subordination. The Platonists grasped the destination but lacked the vehicle: the Incarnation. This framing allowed Christianity to absorb Platonic metaphysics while claiming superiority.
